ig_caption:

name: Kawadoko River Dining (Kifune)
why: Centuries-old summer tradition: eat kaiseki on wooden platforms built over rushing mountain stream, water 30cm below your feet. May-September only. ~$120 dinner
family_fit: Magical for the whole family -- eating over rushing water is unforgettable. The Eizan Railway scenic train journey adds to the experience
booking: Reservation required for dinner at specific restaurants
ig_caption:
The $120 dinner in Japan I couldnât finish  and why Iâd absolutely do it again ð Save this!

This is kawadoko (å·åº) - Japanâs centuries-old summer river dining tradition. Wooden platforms built directly over a rushing mountain stream, where you eat with the water flowing 30cm beneath your feet. From May to late September only.

Where: Kifune, in northern Kyoto

A small mountain village 1.5 hours from central Kyoto. Eizan Railway scenic train through the mountains, then a short bus ride. Worth the journey alone - the village itself is magical even without the dinner.

Why Japanese people obsess over it:

ð¿ 5-10Â°C cooler than central Kyoto in summer (naturally air-conditioned)
ð¿ 1,300-year-old Kifune Shrine
ð¿ Centuries-old tradition dating to Edo period
ð¿ Sound of running water replaces all conversation
ð¿ The platforms are sacred, not just dining

Traditional kaiseki at premium Kibune restaurants runs Â¥10,000-20,000 ($70-140) for dinner. We paid $120 per person at one of the kaiseki spots.

What you actually get:
ð Cold noodles
ð Whole grilled ayu (sweetfish) on a bed of 
stones - Kyotoâs signature summer fish
ð Hamo (pike conger) shabu-shabu
ð Multiple courses of seasonal mountain vegetables and unfamiliar textures
ð Dishes that genuinely challenge Western palates

The food is intentionally subtle, seasonal, and 
deeply tied to the river itself. For Japanese 
diners, this is a centuries-old ritual - eaten 
in near-silence, savored slowly.

Kawadoko isnât about food. Itâs about eating WITH the river, the mountain, the season. Itâs a meditation, not a meal. Once you accept that, the experience becomes profound.

name: Heian Jingu Shrine
why: Colossal iconic orange torii gate and gorgeous expansive imperial-style garden. Vibrant colors and sheer scale are breathtaking
family_fit: The massive orange torii gate is jaw-dropping for kids. Wide open spaces for them to explore
ig_caption:
Stop only visiting a few famous temples in Kyoto! â©ï¸â¨

Most visitors only flock to hyper-famous locations like Kiyomizu-dera or Kinkaku-ji, missing out on the incredible variety and depth of the rest of the city.

Kyoto has so many other magnificent, world-class shrines that are just as legendary and breathtaking.

Here are 3 stunning shrines you need to add to your itinerary to see the full picture:

1. Heian Jingu Shrine ð§¡
Famous for its colossal, iconic orange Torii gate and a gorgeous, expansive imperial-style garden. The sheer scale and vibrant colors of the architecture are absolutely stunning and perfect for photos.

2. Shimogamo Jinja Shrine ð³
One of Kyotoâs oldest World Heritage sites, beautifully nestled inside the sacred "Tadasu no Mori" forest. Walking through these towering, majestic trees offers a deeply spiritual experience rooted in ancient history.

3. Kitano Tenmangu Shrine ð¸
Features spectacular, ornate golden architecture and thousands of beautiful plum trees. Itâs an incredible place to admire raw Japanese history and witness stunning traditional craftsmanship up close.

BEST WAGYU STEAK IN THE WORLD? 

These guys farm their own bread of wagyu called Mogami Beef in north east Japan in Tohokuâs Yamagata Prefecture. Itâs in their third generation of family owners who believe that have perfected the cows diet combining rice and mountain water. 

90% of Mogami beef is ranked A4 or A5 compared to Kobe which is around 80%. 

At the restaurant you can order a 2LB/ 900KG wagyu steak (which is unheard of in Japan) - it costs around Â£130 and includes the sides and would feed 3-4 people!

name: Samoyed Cafe Moffu Harajuku
why: Cute Harajuku cafe where fluffy Samoyed dogs steal the show — staff help with photos, welcoming atmosphere, 1-hour booking
family_fit: Excellent — both kids will love petting fluffy dogs

name: Cafe Reissue
why: Famous for adorable 3D and 2D latte art of your favorite characters — rich coffee, must-try cheesecake, fun creative experience
family_fit: Excellent — kids will love seeing characters drawn in latte art

name: Cafe Hanon Ginza
why: One of Tokyo's best breakfast spots for fluffy Japanese-style pancakes — soft, thick, perfectly cooked with sweet and savory options
family_fit: Excellent — kids love fluffy pancakes, arrive before opening to beat lines

name: Chacha Koubou Kiyosumi
why: Specialty pancake cafe where you watch chefs prepare ultra-fluffy pancakes in front of you — house-made lemon jam adds refreshing balance
family_fit: Great — watching pancakes made live is entertaining for kids

name: Ruru Shibuya
why: Popular cafe famous for floating matcha desserts and layered drinks that look almost too good to eat — modern, stylish, great for photos
family_fit: Good — visually stunning desserts kids will enjoy photographing and eating

name: Kumonocha Cafe
why: Trendy matcha cafe known for signature cloud-shaped matcha drinks and desserts — Instagram-friendly presentation with solid quality
family_fit: Good — fun cloud-shaped drinks appeal to kids

name: The Matcha Tokyo
why: Well-known organic matcha chain with simple, clean menu — great for a quick matcha latte or soft serve while sightseeing
family_fit: Good — easy grab-and-go matcha, multiple locations

name: Atelier Matcha
why: Specialty matcha cafe with premium tea leaves, calm minimal atmosphere — ideal for matcha lovers who want quality over flash
family_fit: Moderate — more of an adult cafe, calm setting

name: Gokago Matcha
why: Traditional-style matcha shop with bold, authentic flavors — classic Japanese sweets, stronger tea taste preferred by locals
family_fit: Moderate — traditional style may be less kid-appealing

name: Kitasando Coffee
why: Popular Tokyo cafe chain with thick cream-topped drinks and minimalist design — good matcha options alongside specialty coffee
family_fit: Good — cream-topped drinks kids will enjoy

name: WeLoveDonut
why: Popular donut shop with soft fluffy texture and creative flavors beyond basic glazed — seasonal specials and beautifully presented designs at $1.50-$3 each
family_fit: Excellent — affordable, fun donut flavors kids will love picking

name: Mipig Cafe
why: Popular animal cafe where you interact with micro pigs in a cozy indoor space — unique and cute, very popular on social media
family_fit: Excellent — both kids will love the micro pigs

name: Cat Cafe MOCHA Lounge
why: Stylish cat cafe with modern lounge atmosphere and plenty of friendly cats — calm break from busy sightseeing
family_fit: Good — relaxing for the whole family, kids love cats

name: Capybara Onsen Moffu Tokyo
why: Unique animal cafe with capybaras relaxing in hot spring-style baths — entertaining, photo-friendly, something you won't find outside Japan
family_fit: Excellent — capybaras in tiny hot tubs, kids will be amazed

name: Cappiness Oshiage Capybara Cafe
why: Unique animal cafe near Tokyo Skytree where you can pet, feed, and photograph friendly capybaras in a calm cozy space
family_fit: Excellent — combine with Skytree visit, kids love capybaras

name: Hiiragi Ginza
why: Well-known sweets shop famous for taiyaki and seasonal kakigori (shaved ice) — classic flavors, local favorite for traditional desserts
family_fit: Great — taiyaki and shaved ice are kid favorites, perfect summer treat

name: Hyosha Mamatoko
why: Specialty shaved ice shop with creative flavor combinations and seasonal menus — soft ice that melts smoothly, fun for dessert lovers
family_fit: Great — creative shaved ice flavors, perfect for August heat

name: Sabo Okuno Shibuya
why: Traditional-style dessert cafe near Shibuya with classic Japanese sweets and shaved ice — popular with locals, no tourist crowds
family_fit: Good — authentic Japanese desserts in a calm setting

name: SG Club Bar
why: World-class cocktail bar with creative, perfectly balanced cocktails — stylish yet relaxed, every drink feels like a work of art
family_fit: Adults only — great for parents' night out

name: Tir Na Nog Ginza
why: Hidden speakeasy perfectly blending creativity and elegance — inventive cocktails with unique matcha and red velvet flavors, magical interior
family_fit: Adults only — great for parents' night out

name: Sky Lounge Stellar Garden
why: 33rd floor lounge with one of Tokyo's best views of Tokyo Tower — calm, romantic, cocktails ¥2,000-3,000, professional service
family_fit: Adults only — great for parents' date night

name: Yakiniku En Shibuya
why: All-you-can-eat Wagyu starting from $12 — great quality meat at fair price near Shibuya Crossing, friendly staff, wagyu karubi highlight
family_fit: Excellent — interactive grilling, incredible value for wagyu, kids love it

name: Osusumeya Shinjuku
why: Lively all-you-can-eat spot for ~2000 yen — unlimited food, energetic atmosphere with young locals, fun and filling, book ahead
family_fit: Good for teens — lively atmosphere, great value

name: Sweets Paradise Shinjuku
why: All-you-can-eat dessert buffet near Shinjuku Station — huge variety of cakes, pies, seasonal treats plus light meals, great after dinner
family_fit: Excellent — kids will go wild at a dessert buffet

name: Sushiro
why: Massive conveyor belt sushi chain with quality plates from ~$1 each — stuff yourself with quality sushi for under $11, hundreds of locations
family_fit: Excellent — kids love grabbing plates off the belt, touchscreen ordering, affordable

name: Kura Sushi
why: Popular conveyor belt sushi chain with plates ~$1 each — touchscreen ordering, fun prize games after collecting plates, hundreds of locations
family_fit: Excellent — kids love the conveyor belt, prize games, and choosing their own plates

name: Ichiran Ramen
why: Most popular ramen chain in Japan — individual booth dining, customizable noodle firmness/broth richness/spice, bowls $5-8
family_fit: Good — individual booths are unique experience, customize to each kid's taste

name: Ippudo Ramen
why: Super popular ramen chain with rich, creamy tonkotsu broth — similar price to Ichiran, different flavor profile (creamier vs bolder)
family_fit: Good — widely loved, accessible flavor for kids

name: Ramenya Shima
why: Legendary ramen spot with only 60 bowls per day — get a ticket when they open and return at booked time, guide says it's on their bucket list
family_fit: Challenging with kids (limited availability, queuing required) but worth it for ramen lovers

name: Nagi Ramen - Golden Gai
why: Must-try ramen tucked in Golden Gai — famous for rich sardine-based broth (niboshi), two types of noodles, open 24 hours, bold but balanced
family_fit: Adventurous — fish-based ramen, cramped Golden Gai setting not ideal for young kids

name: Chuka-Soba Aoba Nakano
why: One of the best ramen spots in Tokyo — rich fish and chicken broth that's different from most ramen, in hip Nakano neighborhood
family_fit: Good — approachable broth flavor, Nakano is fun to explore with kids

name: Gindaco Takoyaki
why: Well-known takoyaki chain — crispy outside, soft inside octopus balls topped with sauce, bonito flakes, green onions, ~$4-5 per serving
family_fit: Excellent — fun street food, kids love watching them being made

name: Gyoza no Osho
why: Popular chain for freshly made gyoza — Japanese dumplings are 10x better than American versions, plates $2-4
family_fit: Excellent — kids love gyoza, cheap and filling

name: Saizeriya
why: Popular Japanese-Italian chain with pasta from $2, pizza, and wine — casual dining with Italian-inspired and Japanese flavors at incredibly low prices
family_fit: Excellent — familiar Italian food, incredibly cheap, kids love pasta

name: Starbucks Reserve Roastery Tokyo
why: One of only seven Reserve Roasteries in the world — set along Meguro River, watch beans roasted in-house, more destination than cafe
family_fit: Great — beautiful multi-floor space, interesting for kids to see coffee production

name: Omoide Yokocho (Memory Lane)
why: Famous narrow alley packed with tiny yakitori shops and old-school izakayas — nostalgic Showa-era atmosphere, classic Tokyo nightlife vibe
family_fit: Atmospheric walk-through for the family, may be smoky/tight for dining with kids

name: Harmonica Yokocho
why: Local favorite drinking alley with small bars and standing eateries — less touristy than Omoide Yokocho, popular with residents
family_fit: More of an adult spot, but atmospheric to walk through

name: Imperial Palace East Gardens
why: Beautiful free gardens with historical Edo Castle remnants — serene atmosphere for leisurely walks and picnics amidst seasonal flowers
family_fit: Good — peaceful stroll, but may bore younger kids quickly

name: Odaiba Seaside Park
why: Locals' favorite date spot on Tokyo Bay — scenic views of Rainbow Bridge and skyline, waterfront stroll, beach, vibrant atmosphere, free
family_fit: Great — kids can play on the beach area, beautiful views, combine with Odaiba attractions

name: Hotel Chinzanso Tokyo (Sea of Clouds)
why: Stunning 'sea of clouds' rolls through the garden several times daily — soft mist among trees and pagoda, dreamy mountain-like scene in the city
family_fit: Magical — kids will love the mist effects, short and free to observe

name: Yurakucho Sanchoku Inshokugai
why: Lively food alley under railway tracks near Yurakucho Station — each eatery specializes in dishes from a different region of Japan, authentic no-frills dining
family_fit: Atmospheric walk-through, casual eating — trains rumbling overhead is exciting for kids

name: Kiyosumi Garden
why: Tranquil Japanese strolling garden with a large pond and stepping stones — peaceful escape from the city, formerly a feudal lord's residence
family_fit: Good — stepping stones on water are fun for kids, but may bore them after 30 min

name: National Art Center Tokyo (NACT)
why: Striking glass-facade museum by Kisho Kurokawa with diverse temporary exhibitions — the building itself is a work of art, free areas to explore
family_fit: Moderate — depends on current exhibition, stunning building to photograph

name: Maxell Aqua Park Shinagawa
why: One-of-a-kind aquarium with stunning light shows, interactive exhibits, and creatively designed tanks — aquatic life meets high-tech art
family_fit: Excellent — kids love aquariums, this one has tech/light shows that make it extra special

name: Art Aquarium Museum
why: Stunning fusion of art, Japanese aesthetics, and vibrant goldfish displays — mesmerizing light installations, immersive atmosphere in Ginza
family_fit: Great — beautiful and unique, kids love the goldfish in artistic tanks

name: Salt and Tobacco Museum
why: Weirdly fascinating museum about salt and tobacco history — ancient salt-making, vintage ads, quirky and educational, only ¥100 admission
family_fit: Good for curious kids — quirky topic, super cheap, short visit

name: Hie Shrine
why: 15th century shrine with bright red torii gates lining the hill path (like Kyoto's Fushimi Inari but in Tokyo) — peaceful, best in morning
family_fit: Great — the tunnel of red torii gates is photogenic and exciting for kids to walk through

name: Gotokuji Temple
why: Birthplace of the maneki-neko (lucky cat) — thousands of small cat statues line the grounds, unique photo spot, quiet and peaceful
family_fit: Excellent — kids will love the thousands of lucky cat statues, great photo ops

name: Nezu Shrine
why: Beautiful shrine with path of red torii gates like Fushimi Inari but much less crowded — spacious, peaceful, famous azalea garden in spring
family_fit: Great — torii gate tunnel is fun to walk through, less touristy

name: Marishiten Tokudaiji Temple
why: Secret temple tucked above the famous Ameyoko Market — climb narrow stairs from the market chaos to find this quiet spiritual pocket of Ueno
family_fit: Fun discovery — kids will love finding a hidden temple above a busy market

name: Flamingo Shimokitazawa
why: Vintage lover's paradise with curated American vintage clothing 1960s-1990s — monthly 'Fill-a-Bag' sale is a treasure hunt for deals
family_fit: Good for 13yo who likes fashion/vintage — fun neighborhood to explore

name: Ginza Tsutaya Books
why: The coolest bookstore in Tokyo with over 60,000 books — great place to reset and relax after a long day of walking, something for everybody
family_fit: Good — beautiful space, books for all interests, cafe inside

name: Jimbocho Book Town
why: Tokyo's 'book town' — packed with old bookstores, cozy cafes, vintage paper shops, rare finds from decades-old manga to antique Japanese prints
family_fit: Interesting for book-loving kids — manga hunting, nostalgic atmosphere

name: Hakone Open-Air Museum
why: Unique blend of art and nature — outdoor sculptures in mountain setting, interactive exhibits, Picasso collection, guide calls it 'refreshing'
family_fit: Excellent — kids can run around outdoor sculptures, climbing play sculpture, Picasso pavilion

name: Hakone Shrine
why: Famous shrine on Lake Ashi with iconic red torii gate standing in the water — one of most photographed spots in Hakone region
family_fit: Great — iconic lakeside torii gate photo, combine with boat ride

name: Lake Ashi Boat Cruise
why: Scenic boat ride on Lake Ashi with stunning views of Mount Fuji — pirate ship-style boats, part of the classic Hakone sightseeing loop
family_fit: Excellent — kids love the pirate ship boats, beautiful scenery

name: Hakone Ropeway / Owakudani Valley
why: Panoramic ropeway ride over volcanic Owakudani Valley — try the famous black eggs boiled in volcanic sulfur springs, said to add 7 years to your life
family_fit: Excellent — gondola ride, volcanic landscape, black eggs are fun novelty for kids

name: Kamakura Great Buddha (Daibutsu)
why: Iconic giant bronze Buddha statue — must-see landmark, you can go inside the hollow statue, about 1 hour from Tokyo
family_fit: Great — impressive giant statue, going inside is cool for kids

name: Tokyo Station Character Street
why: Underground shopping area inside Tokyo Station filled with shops for Pokemon, Hello Kitty, Ghibli, Ultraman — fun for kids and anime fans
family_fit: Excellent — kids will love the themed shops, easy to access from train station

name: Kirby Cafe
why: Nintendo-themed restaurant with creative dishes resembling Kirby characters — playful presentation, themed desserts, fun for gaming fans
family_fit: Excellent — especially for the 13yo gamer, creative food presentation

name: KidZania Tokyo
why: Interactive indoor mini-city where kids role-play real jobs (firefighter, doctor, pilot, chef) and earn KidZania currency — highly organized, educational
family_fit: Perfect for 9yo — one of Tokyo's most unique family attractions, the 13yo may feel too old

name: Tokyo Toy Museum
why: Charming hands-on museum in former elementary school — wooden toys, play areas, interactive exhibits, relaxed environment
family_fit: Better for 9yo — hands-on play, 13yo may be less interested

name: Hamleys Toy Store Ginza
why: One of the largest toy stores in Tokyo — multiple floors, interactive displays, staff demonstrate toys and engage kids with playful activities
family_fit: Excellent for both kids — interactive and fun even if you don't buy anything

name: Pokemon Center Mega Tokyo
why: One of the largest Pokemon stores in Japan — massive merchandise, plush toys, games, collectibles, large character statues, immersive displays
family_fit: Excellent — both kids will love browsing the massive Pokemon collection

name: Pokemon Center Shibuya
why: Modern stylish Pokemon store inside Parco — exclusive merchandise, character statues, large digital Mewtwo display, unique designs
family_fit: Great — can combine with Nintendo Store in same building

name: Doraemon Museum (Fujiko F. Fujio Museum)
why: Dedicated to the creator of Doraemon — original artwork, character displays, themed exhibits, cafe and gift shop, fun for anime fans
family_fit: Good if kids know Doraemon — charming and creative, about 30 min from Shinjuku

name: Kiddy Land Harajuku
why: One of Tokyo's most famous toy stores — multi-floor shop packed with Hello Kitty, Rilakkuma, Snoopy, Pokemon, huge variety of character goods
family_fit: Excellent — both kids will love browsing, great for character souvenirs

name: LEGOLAND Discovery Center Tokyo
why: Indoor attraction with interactive building zones, small rides, creative play areas, miniature LEGO Tokyo landmarks
family_fit: Great for 9yo especially — building zones and rides, 13yo may find it young

name: Tokyo Dome City
why: Large entertainment complex with amusement park, shopping, restaurants — famous Thunder Dolphin roller coaster through buildings, free entry (pay per ride)
family_fit: Excellent — roller coaster for 13yo, rides for 9yo, free to enter and explore

name: Sunshine City Ikebukuro
why: Massive indoor complex with aquarium, Pokemon Center, shopping, restaurants, entertainment — everything connected indoors, easy to spend several hours
family_fit: Excellent — multiple attractions under one roof, aquarium is great for kids

name: Nintendo Store Shibuya
why: Official flagship Nintendo shop — collectibles, apparel, plush toys featuring Mario, Zelda, Splatoon, large character statues and themed displays
family_fit: Excellent — both kids will love it, especially gamers

name: GIGO Arcades
why: Ultimate Tokyo arcade — 6 floors of video games, rhythm games, claw machines, competitive fighting games, much bigger than Western game centers
family_fit: Excellent for both kids — claw machines, rhythm games, racing simulators

name: Super Potato Retro Game Store
why: Famous retro gaming shop specializing in classic games and consoles — shelves of nostalgic titles, small retro arcade area, fun for gaming fans
family_fit: Great for 13yo gamer — browsing classic games, playable retro arcade

name: Nakano Broadway
why: Famous shopping complex for anime, manga, collectibles — rare figures, vintage toys, retro atmosphere, also has secondhand luxury watch shops
family_fit: Good for older kid — treasure hunting for anime goods, unique finds

name: Gachapon Hall Akihabara
why: One of the largest collections of capsule toy machines in Tokyo — hundreds of machines with anime characters, animals, quirky Japanese designs
family_fit: Excellent — both kids will love the surprise element, inexpensive souvenirs

name: Animate Ikebukuro
why: Biggest anime retail chain's flagship store — multi-floor with manga, figures, character goods, exclusive merchandise, easily an hour of browsing
family_fit: Great if kids are into anime/manga — massive selection

name: Sushi Making Tokyo (Asakusa)
why: Hands-on sushi-making classes with professional instructors — make nigiri and rolls, learn about ingredients and techniques, beginner-friendly
family_fit: Excellent — interactive, educational, both kids will love making their own sushi

name: Tokyo Metro Museum
why: Fun educational museum about Tokyo's subway — train simulators, railway displays, interactive exhibits on how trains operate
family_fit: Great for 9yo especially — driver simulators and model trains

name: Kewpie Mayo Terrace
why: Interactive museum about Japan's famous Kewpie mayonnaise — learn about the brand, see how it's made, fun food-related exhibits
family_fit: Good — short but entertaining, especially for food-curious families

name: Tokyo Metropolitan Government Building (Free Observation + Light Show)
why: Free observation deck with wide city views (sometimes Mt. Fuji) PLUS nighttime projection mapping light show on the massive facade — both free
family_fit: Excellent — free views during day, light show at night is mesmerizing for kids

name: Tokyo Skytree
why: Tallest structure in Japan — panoramic views stretching across the skyline, can see Mt. Fuji on clear days, dramatic observation deck
family_fit: Great — kids love the height, glass floor sections are thrilling

name: Roppongi Hills Sky Deck
why: Both indoor and outdoor rooftop viewing — incredible skyline views from Mori Tower plus open-air deck, beautiful views of Tokyo Tower nearby
family_fit: Great — outdoor deck is thrilling, beautiful sunset views

name: KITTE Marunouchi (6th Floor Terrace)
why: Free overlooking terrace with great view of the historic Tokyo Station red-brick building — especially beautiful at night, recommended by guide's local author
family_fit: Quick free stop — great nighttime photo of Tokyo Station

name: Kawagoe (Little Edo)
why: Called 'Little Edo' — well-preserved historic warehouse district, samurai residence, feels like going back in time, under 1 hour from Shinjuku
family_fit: Great — historical time travel feeling, candy street is fun for kids

name: Shimokitazawa neighborhood
why: Popular thrifting and vintage fashion neighborhood — curated shops, bohemian vibe, great for exploring on foot between Shibuya and Shinjuku
family_fit: Good for 13yo who likes fashion — vintage shops, quirky cafes, youthful energy

name: Daikanyama neighborhood
why: Ultra-stylish neighborhood 10 min from Shibuya — beautifully designed cafes, boutique shops, relaxed shopping streets, slow mornings and good coffee
family_fit: More of a parent vibe, but Tsutaya T-Site bookstore here is interesting for all

name: Shibamata neighborhood
why: Frozen-in-time area with retro 1910s shopping street leading to Taishakuten Temple with intricate wooden carvings — rare glimpse into old Tokyo
family_fit: Interesting cultural detour — retro shopping street has nostalgic charm

name: Nikko (Toshogu Shrine + Kegon Falls)
why: UNESCO World Heritage site with grand Toshogu Shrine and stunning Kegon Falls — unforgettable mix of nature, history, and spirituality, 2 hours from Tokyo
family_fit: Great if you have a free day — beautiful nature, impressive shrine, waterfalls kids love

name: Capcom Store Tokyo
why: Official Capcom merchandise — Street Fighter, Monster Hunter, Resident Evil collectibles, figures, apparel, exclusive items for gamers
family_fit: Good for 13yo gamer — Monster Hunter and Street Fighter are popular with teens

name: Jump Shop
why: Shonen Jump merchandise — Dragon Ball, One Piece, Naruto, Jujutsu Kaisen goods, exclusive items, character displays
family_fit: Excellent for both kids if they watch anime — Dragon Ball and Naruto are kid favorites

name: Purikura Photo Booths
why: Japanese decorated photo booth machines — take photos then add filters, stickers, drawings, print as small photo stickers, beloved by Japanese teens
family_fit: Excellent — both kids will love decorating photos, fun cheap souvenir

name: Sumo Practice Session (Free at Sumo Stable)
why: Free sumo wrestling practice at a sumo stable (beya) — watch rikishi train early morning, unforgettable cultural experience, must be respectful
family_fit: Unique cultural experience — early morning timing may be tough, but memorable for the whole family

name: Tsukiji Outer Market
why: Must-visit for food lovers — free to explore, vendors selling fresh seafood, produce, street food, small eateries, go first thing in the morning
family_fit: Excellent — sampling street food is fun for kids, tuna and seafood treats

name: Toyosu Fish Market
why: Modern high-tech replacement for inner Tsukiji — tuna auction is a major spectacle, best sushi restaurants from old Tsukiji moved here, clean indoor facility
family_fit: Great — tuna auction is exciting for kids, great sushi restaurants inside

name: Daikoku Parking Area Car Meet
why: Famous gathering spot for Japanese car enthusiasts — modified sports cars, classic vehicles, rare imports on certain nights, Japan's unique car culture
family_fit: Great for 13yo into cars — requires taxi/car to reach, more of an evening activity

name: Eki Stamp Collecting (Train Station Stamps)
why: Free activity — special ink stamps at train stations and attractions with unique designs, collect throughout trip as a fun free souvenir in a notebook
family_fit: Excellent — turns every train station into a treasure hunt, both kids will love it

name: Matsuya
why: Popular fast-food chain with tasty gyudon (beef bowl), curry, and grilled sets — $2-5 per meal with free miso soup, reliable quick local meal
family_fit: Good — fast, cheap, filling, familiar enough for kids

name: Gusto
why: Popular family restaurant with affordable meals $3-10 — pasta, hamburg steak, curry, comfortable seating with self-serve drink bar
family_fit: Excellent — family restaurant designed for families, self-serve drinks, familiar food

name: A10 Bar Ebisu
why: Featured secret bar in Ebisu — one of Tokyo's most kept secret bars, highlighted as a full-page spread in the guide
family_fit: Adults only

name: VIRTU Bar (Four Seasons Otemachi)
why: Refined cocktail bar in Four Seasons Hotel with breathtaking skyline views — expert bartenders, personalized cocktails, one of Tokyo's finest bars
family_fit: Adults only — luxury experience for parents' night out

name: Hotto Motto
why: Japan's go-to bento chain — freshly made affordable boxed meals with karaage, grilled fish, curry, perfect for takeout convenience
family_fit: Good — easy grab-and-go bento, kids like picking their own meal box

name: Koenji Awa Odori Dance Festival
why: Free dance festival in August with teams of dancers parading down streets — sit curbside and enjoy the show, one of Tokyo's most exciting summer events
family_fit: Excellent if timing works — free, exciting, great atmosphere for kids to experience Japanese festival culture

name: Face Records Shimokitazawa
why: Vinyl lover's paradise specializing in rare jazz, soul, and funk records — intimate store with new and secondhand vinyl from different eras
family_fit: Niche — music lover parent activity, browse while exploring Shimokitazawa

name: Ana-hachimangu Shrine
why: Lesser-known shrine near Waseda popular for prayers for luck, success, and safe travel — calm and local compared to tourist spots
family_fit: Good — quiet shrine for a peaceful moment, prayers for safe travel fitting for the trip

name: Yasukuni Shrine
why: Large Shinto shrine near central Tokyo with wide open grounds — visited by locals during festivals and cherry blossom season, museum optional
family_fit: Good for a walk — wide grounds to explore, near other attractions
